title: Trigonometry
blurb: Exact values, triangles from triples, equations on the circle, and the laws — trig in every exam costume.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\sin 150°$.
variants.explanation: $150°$ lies in quadrant II, reference angle $30°$. In QII sine is positive, cosine negative: $\sin 150° = \frac{1}{2}$.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\cos 120°$.
variants.explanation: $120°$ lies in quadrant II, reference angle $60°$. In QII sine is positive, cosine negative: $\cos 120° = - \frac{1}{2}$.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\sin 120°$.
variants.explanation: $120°$ lies in quadrant II, reference angle $60°$. In QII sine is positive, cosine negative: $\sin 120° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

variants.statement: Evaluate exactly: $\cos 135°$.
variants.explanation: $135°$ lies in quadrant II, reference angle $45°$. In QII sine is positive, cosine negative: $\cos 135° = - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$.

forms.title: Degrees ↔ radians
forms.skill: Multiply degrees by π/180; reduce the fraction.

variants.statement: Convert $150°$ to radians.
variants.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$: $150° = \frac{150\pi}{180} = \dfrac{5\pi}{6}$.

variants.statement: Convert $120°$ to radians.
variants.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$: $120° = \frac{120\pi}{180} = \dfrac{2\pi}{3}$.

variants.statement: Convert $135°$ to radians.
variants.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$: $135° = \frac{135\pi}{180} = \dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.

variants.statement: Convert $240°$ to radians.
variants.explanation: Multiply by $\frac{\pi}{180}$: $240° = \frac{240\pi}{180} = \dfrac{4\pi}{3}$.

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $10$ and an acute angle of $30°$. Find the side OPPOSITE the $30°$ angle.
variants.explanation: Opposite over hypotenuse is sine: side $= 10\sin 30° = 5$. (Using cosine would give the ADJACENT side.)

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $8$ and an acute angle of $60°$. Find the side OPPOSITE the $60°$ angle.
variants.explanation: Opposite over hypotenuse is sine: side $= 8\sin 60° = 4 \sqrt{3}$. (Using cosine would give the ADJACENT side.)

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $14$ and an acute angle of $30°$. Find the side OPPOSITE the $30°$ angle.
variants.explanation: Opposite over hypotenuse is sine: side $= 14\sin 30° = 7$. (Using cosine would give the ADJACENT side.)

variants.statement: A right triangle has hypotenuse $10$ and an acute angle of $45°$. Find the side OPPOSITE the $45°$ angle.
variants.explanation: Opposite over hypotenuse is sine: side $= 10\sin 45° = 5 \sqrt{2}$. (Using cosine would give the ADJACENT side.)

forms.title: From sin to cos and tan
forms.skill: Build the triangle from the triple; read the other ratios.

variants.statement: An acute angle $\alpha$ has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{3}{5}$. Find $\cos\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Draw the right triangle: opposite $3$, hypotenuse $5$ ⟹ adjacent $= \sqrt{5^2 - 3^2} = 4$ (the 3-4-5 triple). Then $\cos\alpha = \frac{4}{5}$.

variants.statement: An acute angle $\alpha$ has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{5}{13}$. Find $\tan\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Draw the right triangle: opposite $5$, hypotenuse $13$ ⟹ adjacent $= \sqrt{13^2 - 5^2} = 12$ (the 5-12-13 triple). Then $\tan\alpha = \frac{5}{12}$.

variants.statement: An acute angle $\alpha$ has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{8}{17}$. Find $\cos\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Draw the right triangle: opposite $8$, hypotenuse $17$ ⟹ adjacent $= \sqrt{17^2 - 8^2} = 15$ (the 8-15-17 triple). Then $\cos\alpha = \frac{15}{17}$.

variants.statement: An acute angle $\alpha$ has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{7}{25}$. Find $\tan\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Draw the right triangle: opposite $7$, hypotenuse $25$ ⟹ adjacent $= \sqrt{25^2 - 7^2} = 24$ (the 7-24-25 triple). Then $\tan\alpha = \frac{7}{24}$.

forms.title: Solving sin x = k on one turn
forms.skill: Two mirror solutions per turn; supplementary for positive k.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = \dfrac{1}{2}$ have on $[0°; 360°)$ — and what is their SUM?
variants.explanation: Sine takes each value in $(-1; 1)$ twice per turn: at $x = 30°$ and $x = 150°$ (mirror pair). Sum: $180°$.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ have on $[0°; 360°)$ — and what is their SUM?
variants.explanation: Sine takes each value in $(-1; 1)$ twice per turn: at $x = 60°$ and $x = 120°$ (mirror pair). Sum: $180°$.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$ have on $[0°; 360°)$ — and what is their SUM?
variants.explanation: Sine takes each value in $(-1; 1)$ twice per turn: at $x = 45°$ and $x = 135°$ (mirror pair). Sum: $180°$.

variants.statement: How many solutions does $\sin x = -\dfrac{1}{2}$ have on $[0°; 360°)$ — and what is their SUM?
variants.explanation: Sine takes each value in $(-1; 1)$ twice per turn: at $x = 210°$ and $x = 330°$ (mirror pair). Sum: $540°$.

forms.title: The Pythagorean identity
forms.skill: sin² + cos² = 1, so 1 − cos² is sin².

variants.statement: If $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{4}{5}$, evaluate $1 - \cos^2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Pythagorean identity: $1 - \cos^2\alpha = \sin^2\alpha$. With $\cos\alpha = \frac{4}{5}$: $1 - \frac{16}{25} = \frac{9}{25}$.

variants.statement: If $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{12}{13}$, evaluate $1 - \cos^2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Pythagorean identity: $1 - \cos^2\alpha = \sin^2\alpha$. With $\cos\alpha = \frac{12}{13}$: $1 - \frac{144}{169} = \frac{25}{169}$.

variants.statement: If $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{15}{17}$, evaluate $1 - \cos^2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Pythagorean identity: $1 - \cos^2\alpha = \sin^2\alpha$. With $\cos\alpha = \frac{15}{17}$: $1 - \frac{225}{289} = \frac{64}{289}$.

variants.statement: If $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{21}{29}$, evaluate $1 - \cos^2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Pythagorean identity: $1 - \cos^2\alpha = \sin^2\alpha$. With $\cos\alpha = \frac{21}{29}$: $1 - \frac{441}{841} = \frac{400}{841}$.

forms.title: The double-angle formula
forms.skill: sin 2α = 2 sin α cos α — never just double.

variants.statement: An acute angle has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{3}{5}$ and $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{4}{5}$. Find $\sin 2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Double angle: $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{4}{5} = \frac{24}{25}$. Doubling the ANGLE never doubles the sine.

variants.statement: An acute angle has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{5}{13}$ and $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{12}{13}$. Find $\sin 2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Double angle: $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{5}{13} \cdot \frac{12}{13} = \frac{120}{169}$. Doubling the ANGLE never doubles the sine.

variants.statement: An acute angle has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{8}{17}$ and $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{15}{17}$. Find $\sin 2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Double angle: $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{8}{17} \cdot \frac{15}{17} = \frac{240}{289}$. Doubling the ANGLE never doubles the sine.

variants.statement: An acute angle has $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{7}{25}$ and $\cos\alpha = \dfrac{24}{25}$. Find $\sin 2\alpha$.
variants.explanation: Double angle: $\sin 2\alpha = 2\sin\alpha\cos\alpha = 2 \cdot \frac{7}{25} \cdot \frac{24}{25} = \frac{336}{625}$. Doubling the ANGLE never doubles the sine.

forms.title: The law of cosines
forms.skill: c² = a² + b² − 2ab·cos C; 60° and 120° keep it exact.

variants.statement: A triangle has sides $5$ and $8$ with a $60°$ angle between them. Find the third side.
variants.explanation: Law of cosines: $c^2 = 5^2 + 8^2 - 2 \cdot 5 \cdot 8\cos 60° = 25 + 64 - 80 \cdot (\frac{1}{2}) = 49$, so $c = 7$.

variants.statement: A triangle has sides $3$ and $5$ with a $120°$ angle between them. Find the third side.
variants.explanation: Law of cosines: $c^2 = 3^2 + 5^2 - 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 5\cos 120° = 9 + 25 - 30 \cdot (- \frac{1}{2}) = 49$, so $c = 7$.

variants.statement: A triangle has sides $7$ and $8$ with a $60°$ angle between them. Find the third side.
variants.explanation: Law of cosines: $c^2 = 7^2 + 8^2 - 2 \cdot 7 \cdot 8\cos 60° = 49 + 64 - 112 \cdot (\frac{1}{2}) = 57$, so $c = \sqrt{57}$.

variants.statement: A triangle has sides $5$ and $8$ with a $120°$ angle between them. Find the third side.
